speaker: interviewer
text: 1996, and we're in Sydney, Australia. And camera is ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Selien, how did it feel to leave-- did you leave your father in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible, but what could you do? I had three kids. I had to have a future for my children. And in my opinion, there was not much future in those days in Holland. So that was one of reasons we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the Dutch people towards the Jewish people after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good, most of them were good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the trip to Australia like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Horrible. I was sick four and a half weeks on the boat. [LAUGHS] Don't ask me. Never again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the boat you came on? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was on that boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friends of ours, also with three kids, and the four of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides you, what were the other passengers? What-- what-- were they refugees, or was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Just a lot of farmers who wanted to get a better future. Because Holland was very overcrowded. And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] a lot of refugees too. But in a way, it was a good trip. But I was sick all the way. So for me, it was no good. But I was glad I left Holland. I couldn't stand it anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- how did you find Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was never homesick, never. And we had a lot of troubles. And my husband became sick, after-effect from the camp. And he had heart troubles, he had back troubles from the bashing, what they did with him, with, with the-- guns on his back and all that. But I never was sorry I left, no. No.

speaker: interviewee
text: He started off at General Motors, panel beating, refrigerators. And because he was a tradesman, he got straight away more money than anybody else. But then the winter came, and the refrigerator sales went down. And so a lot of people were kicked out. But he was allowed to stay. But then he had to go and work on the cars, panel beating. And that was not so bad. But that was-- how you call that, on moving--

speaker: interviewee
text: Production line, and he couldn't take that. So after a while-- and then he started on Sundays or on Saturdays when he had off, he did odd jobs. And-- and that built up after a while. And so he went in the building trade, and worked for several people. And then in-- we came here in '54, and in '55 I got sick at work, and I had to have my first operation here. And my boss drove me home, and I went to [? Lewisham ?] Hospital. And I got a big operation there. And then when I came home, I couldn't go to work, so I worked from home. I did alterations for people. And he started for himself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we-- we saved up from what I was earning, and we bought our first car. But when I'm in the hospital we had to cancel at first, because we didn't have enough money, because I didn't bring any more money in. It was five people, it cost a lot of money to live. But eventually we did it. And then I lived for 10 years in a flat on the other side of the bay here. And then they start building here. And we met a couple on board of the ship. We're still friends. They live in Canberra. And they told us about here the development. And then we came here to have a look. And my husband said, no. I can't afford to buy a house. I'm not looking, and blah, blah, blah. And I said, but I want to have a look. So we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it turned out, we bought a piece of concrete here for $50-- [LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE] 50 pounds-- pounds it was. And then we waited till the house was ready. So then-- and then in the meantime, I talked to the manager, [? West ?] Construction Holdings is that whole project. And I said, have you got some work for us? So, he said, well, the quotes are all out. But by the next time, I'll keep it in mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got work from them. And that built up. And then we had a factory in Botany. And eventually we had 10, 12 men working there. So we did all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Selien, how did you find the Australians reacted with Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the Australian Jews are completely different to the European, sorry to say. But it's true. And they have no idea what went on. I had not long ago a big conversation with a lady about that. And she was annoyed with me. Because I told her, you don't know nothing. Yes, we know all about it. I said, no, you don't. And I don't take it on to you, because I can imagine you didn't live there, so you don't know what went on in Europe. And nobody knows, unless you have been there. But to think all the time, you'll be picked up and destroyed, is not such a pleasant feeling. I tell you that. When the war started, when the Germans came in, my father went to the coast with my uncle, and arranged a boat to bring us to England. But my mother was scared of everything. So no way I was allowed to go. Then he said-- I was engaged then. And then he said, well, then she goes. She's got a whole life ahead of her. No, I was not allowed. Another opportunity I had-- I had a girlfriend whose father had connection in France. And there was part of France what was not occupied. And he got a visitor from that part. And he told my father, if he wanted that woman can take me to France, and then I'll be free. No way. My mother was so scared for everything. And because I was an only child, I was not allowed to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to go at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, anything to escape the German. So-- and it was the same when I was-- when I came free from jail, straight away I went into hiding again. And through the last bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Selien, do you think about your experiences a lot today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. Look, I'll tell you something. That is what I tell my children. That is something what left a mark on your whole life. And you can never forget. It fades, but that's all. And occasionally, you talk to somebody. It always comes up. And sometimes my children don't understand. Mom, forget about it. How can you forget about that? It's impossible. And I was lucky I wasn't in a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do-- do you-- do you think those experiences impacted how you brought up your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: My children say yes. They said I rubbed off on them. But they don't want it, which I can see. I mean it's not a pleasant thing to think about. But they were involved, not Philip, but the other two. But they don't want to know about it. I'm surprised that they're coming. I'll tell you that, [LAUGHS] especially the-- the eldest two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Selien, before we finish the interview, is there anything that you'd like to talk about that perhaps we've forgotten to cover in the interview?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your husband ever talk about his experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never. He never wanted to talk about it, no. Never.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he-- he didn't want to talk about it-- he didn't want to-- to be remembered, no. Occasionally he said something to me, but not to anybody else, no. Not the children, nothing, no. He was not the type to speak out.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he-- when did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 years before that, he got heart trouble. And then he went worse. Every time we had an attack, he went worse. So at one stage, every few nights I drove him to Prince Henry Hospital. And they knew me already. When we came in, oh, you're there again. Yes. So one day, the doctor came. He was in bed. And he walked out. And he said to me, sorry, Mrs. Abram. There's not much hope left. I said, you have to do something. He's too young. To make it short, he said the only thing what is possible, but was here in the very early stages, is bypass. And the only one who did it was Dr. Windsor, Mr. Windsor. But because he was in Prince Henry, and a cardiologist had now done in St. Vincent's, he couldn't do the operation. So he had to come home again. And then from here, being a few days at home again, we actually got him in St. Vincent's, and he was one of the first ones, not the very first one, but one of the first ones who had the bypass operation. What took a whole day, 11 doctors, Dr. Chang was one of them, Dr. Windsor, and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and then he was good for a while. And-- and that lasted exactly 10 years, and then the trouble started again. And then they couldn't do anymore. So it went worse. For 10 years, he couldn't work anymore. And Philip was overseas. We had a factory, and Philip was overseas. And because he is an architect, he was the only one who had a bit of knowledge about what we were doing. So I had to call him back, and take care of the factory. I did the book work, but I never was involved in the work. That's not for woman's work, you know, building cabinets.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then Philip came back, and he was something-- five or six years in the factory. But he hated it. Because that was not his scene. So one day he came home. He said, Dad, I'm sorry. I'm packing in. And so he packed in. But the trouble was the factory was not from us, it was rented. So we couldn't sell the business, because you can't sell customers. We couldn't sell the tools, so we got next to nothing. [SIGHS] Selien, my last question to you is, if you had a message to pass on to your children, and grandchildren, and future generations, now that more than 50 years have passed since the end of the war, what would that be?

speaker: interviewee
text: To let it never happen again, because that is the worst what can happen to somebody. No matter how bad or how good they are, that's the worst what can happen in your life. You can lose a partner, you can have bad luck. But that is something what is indescribable. You can't tell. You can't tell what you feel. And that's inside, and it is-- what can I say? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A sore that never goes away. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Selien, would you like to introduce this large group that's with you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right side is Paul.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the middle is Philip. And on the other side is Karel. That is my daughter-in-law, Freda; and David and Sasha, my grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there's a whole group that aren't here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Philip, would you like to say something about this process or your mom today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'd like to say we're very pleased that Mum has gone through the process, and there was some reluctance early on. And obviously there's been pressure brought to bear by-- by all and sundry. And we're very pleased that we've got a record of-- of some of Mum's recollections. We're obviously very sad that we don't have a record of our other relatives' recollections. In fact, we don't have some of our other relatives here. And we think it's a very commendable process, and we're very pleased to be part of it. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Phil said it, more or less. Kind of it all in a nutshell. I don't think there's much more to add.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [LAUGHTER] Well, I think that my father was right, and I think it's a really good thing that-- that Spielberg's doing. And I think that these people's memories should be remembered, in a good way, not a bad way.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really have anything to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think of your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Daughter-in-law, I got it wrong. See, I've got it wrong for history. No, I think it's great. I think the whole thing Spielberg has done is great. And I've sat with my parents, and been through their interviews, and I think it's wonderful that it's all being captured on film.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, just for the record, which-- better clear up whose children Sasha and David are.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're my children, mine and Philip's children.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll just add something. I think I've raised this before. But one side of the whole Holocaust situation, people like Freda and myself, who would not been here had it not been for the Holocaust. That obviously doesn't make it any more palatable. But it does put a different slant on some of the children. Thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I can relate to that too. Selien, before we finish off, is there something you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. [LAUGHS] I'm glad this is over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Well, I'll finish it off by saying thank you. It's not an easy thing to share your past like that. And thank you very much for sharing it with us today. And I know that your children and your grandchildren and your great grandchildren will only benefit from-- from having that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Selien, can you tell me about these pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are my grandparents, from my father's side. My grandfather died when I was about four years old. And my grandmother was taken away in, I think, '42 to a camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name. And my grandfather was [? Levi ?] Samuel Koperberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he do? Do you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where these photos were taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember her looking like this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. She was a lovely lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get these photos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Last year, by going to the nurse home in my uncle's place in Holland. And then they made a copy for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was in 1995 you got them?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Hessel Goldberg, the father-in-law from my husband's first wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's Philip Abram's wife, first wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to the markets with whatever there was to sell.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to him in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken away, and so it was wife?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this photo taken, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Holland, in Amsterdam, but I don't know where.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know when it was taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about these pictures, Selien.

speaker: interviewee
text: Miethe Prins, her maiden name; and my father, Philip Koperberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when these photographs were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It must have been my father's photo-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know, maybe '38 or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother, was it at the same time?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that how you remember her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I made that dress for her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Black lace, fancy-- fancy front and collar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was these photos taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The left side is my mother, and next to her is her sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know who the little girl is in the front?

speaker: interviewee
text: The little girl was a neighbor. Her surname was [? Hoberts, ?] but I can't remember her first name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Zandvoort, in Holland, a holiday, a beach resort.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- did your aunt survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was taken away on the 7th of August, '42 with her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know what-- did I ask you what year this was taken, this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea, sorry. I don't think so. You got it? You've got a date there? Hang on. Oh '39, it must be on-- on the back.

speaker: interviewee
text: That mini-- that's me. And that was taken, I think, in '39 or '40, just when the war started.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, mixed, no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Selien, tell me the story behind this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a curfew in Holland. So you were not allowed after 8 o'clock at night. So I stayed over with my friends. And that was my second husband, and his first wife. And on that photo, his wife is sitting on the left, and my first husband is standing behind her. On the right, I'm sitting with Karel on my lap, and my second husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so let's just get some names. On the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Karel Abram. Selien Abram, Selien Koperberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken, the address, do you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Selien, can you tell me how you got this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: The neighbor opposite-- it was very narrow street-- made the picture when we were sitting for the window. And that's how we got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you seeing when you looked out of that window? Do you remember what you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. We were just sitting there, and that man said, I'll make a picture of you. So he make a picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain this card please, Selien?

speaker: interviewee
text: In of war, you were not allowed to have lights on your windows, on your bike, or whatever. And we fabricated covers for the bike lights. And we sold it at the market. And my first husband, Heyman Waas, stood in the market with those covers. And there he needed a certificate for.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was issued in 1937 it says on the card.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm in the picture, and my first husband. That was my wedding picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And just give me the names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Selien Abram, or Selien Koperberg, and Heyman Waas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was it taken, the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, I think, in front of the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us what you wore to your wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Mexico, and get that hat. [LAUGHTER] No, it was a very expensive hat, special made.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this wedding photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Bluma Goldberg, Karel's mother, marrying Philip Abram.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and who's the little boy in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the little boy, I think, is her little brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to the wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is Karel Abram and his mother, Bluma Goldberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think he was about one year old, in '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when was he given away?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was about one and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must be taken just before he went in hiding, I suppose.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year would that have been? 194--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where would that have been taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape 4 of the interview with Selien Abram. It's the 12th of July, 1996. And we're in Sydney, [INTERPOSING VOICES] Australia.
